summary: Stuart Russell discusses the limitations of current deep learning models, the massive economic incentives driving AGI development, and the existential risks of the 'standard model' of AI which relies on fixed objectives. To solve the alignment problem, he proposes 'assistance games' where AI systems are explicitly uncertain about human preferences. He also advocates for strict regulatory 'red lines' and hardware-level enforcement to prevent rogue actors from deploying unsafe AI.

principles.statement: AI systems must act in the best interest of humans, but be explicitly uncertain about what those interests are.
principles.rationale: If an AI is uncertain about the objective, it will behave cautiously, defer to humans, and allow itself to be switched off to avoid messing with the universe in ways we don't want.
principles.source_quote: machines have to act in the best interest of humans... but the important thing is those machines are explicitly uncertain about what those human interests are

principles.statement: We must shift from trying to make AI safe after the fact, to only making safe AI from the start.
principles.rationale: Building an AI system first and then trying to figure out how to secure it is an impossible path that will lead to a loss of control over our future.
principles.source_quote: rather than thinking about making AI safe which sort of means making Ai and then thinking about how to make it safe which I think cannot be done we have to think about making only safe AI

principles.statement: The onus of proving safety must be on the AI developers, enforced by strict 'red lines'.
principles.rationale: Just like aviation or nuclear power, developers shouldn't be allowed to release systems they don't understand without proving they won't cross defined, obviously unsafe boundaries.
principles.source_quote: the onus of proof is on the developer to show that their system is not going to cross these red lines as a condition of Market access

frameworks.name: Assistance Games
frameworks.when_to_apply: When designing the core objective function and interaction model of an AI system to ensure alignment with human preferences.
steps: Define a game-theoretic decision problem involving at least one human and one machine.
Assume the human has preferences about the future, even if they are incomplete or unknown to the human themselves.
Assign the machine the job of helping satisfy the human's preferences.
Initialize the machine with explicit uncertainty about what those preferences actually are.
Allow the machine to act cautiously, defer to humans, and accept being switched off as it learns the true preferences.
frameworks.source_quote: we can turn this into a mathematical framework that we call an assistance game so it falls within the economic framework of Game Theory

frameworks.name: Proof Carrying Code for Hardware Regulation
frameworks.when_to_apply: When attempting to prevent rogue actors (the 'Dr. Evil problem') from deploying unsafe AI software.
steps: Require software objects to come with a mathematical proof of safety properties.
Design hardware to quickly and immediately check this proof.
Have the hardware refuse to run software objects that lack a valid proof of safety.
Have software objects refuse to run on hardware that isn't checking the proof.
frameworks.source_quote: proof carrying code means that the software object comes with a proof of whatever property you need it to have and that proof can be checked immediately and quickly by the hardware

mental_models.name: The King Midas Problem
mental_models.description: A metaphor for the failure of the 'standard model' of AI where a fixed objective is specified. The system optimizes the objective literally, leading to unintended and catastrophic consequences.
mental_models.example: King Midas asked for everything he touched to turn to gold, which resulted in his food and family turning to gold, leading to his starvation.

mental_models.name: The Bowl of Fruit / Alcohol Analogy
mental_models.description: A metaphor for our current understanding of Large Language Models. We have stumbled upon a powerful effect through trial and error without understanding the underlying scientific principles.
mental_models.example: 6 or 7,000 years ago someone left a bowl of fruit out in the sun and then came back and drank it and got completely drunk... we have no idea what's going on and how it works that's sort of the situation we're in right now

mental_models.name: Breeding Bigger Birds
mental_models.description: An analogy for the current approach of scaling up deep learning models without understanding their internal mechanics, contrasting it with rigorous engineering.
mental_models.example: Imagine if the airplane manufacturers had decided instead of doing all the engineering and the calculations of lift and drag... had decided just to build or breed bigger and bigger birds

heuristics: If an AI system is explicitly uncertain about human preferences, it will act cautiously and allow itself to be switched off.
Hardware is the only effective regulator for rogue AI actors because software is infinitely copyable and transmitted at the speed of light.

signature_quotes.text: we're in this very odd situation where we've made a scientific advance but we don't know what it is
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the lack of understanding of the internal principles of large language models like GPT-4.

signature_quotes.text: rather than thinking about making AI safe which sort of means making Ai and then thinking about how to make it safe which I think cannot be done we have to think about making only safe AI
signature_quotes.context: Concluding his talk on the necessary paradigm shift in AI development.

signature_quotes.text: the better the AI systems solves this problem the happier we are going to be because that basic issue of misalignment does not arise
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the benefit of using Assistance Games over the standard model of AI.

signature_quotes.text: what we actually need is the hardware to be the regulator here
signature_quotes.context: Addressing the 'Dr. Evil problem' and the impossibility of policing infinitely copyable software.

anti_patterns.description: The Standard Model of AI (specifying fixed objectives for the machine to optimize).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is impossible to specify the objective correctly in the real world, leading to the King Midas problem where the AI optimizes for the wrong thing to the detriment of humanity.

anti_patterns.description: Training Large Language Models simply to copy human behavior.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It does not specify an objective or align the system with human interests, and the systems fail to learn underlying concepts (like arithmetic or the concept of a 'group' in Go), acting more like massive lookup tables.

anti_patterns.description: Scaling up deep learning circuits to solve NP-hard problems.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Because deep learning circuits are linear time computation systems, solving NP-hard problems would require exponentially large circuits and exponential amounts of training data, which is physically impossible.
